extra.reasoning: This is a state machine. The safety property requires globally(opted_out implies not sent): no reachable state may have opted_out=true and sent=true simultaneously. Examining how sent can become true: t2 (send_attempt if not opted_out) sets sent:=true but only when opted_out=false; t3 (send_attempt if opted_out) sets sent:=false; t4 (tick) sets sent:=false. The dangerous rule is t5: on send_attempt if (opted_out and ticks>=3): sent:=true. This explicitly sets sent=true while opted_out is true, directly producing the violating state. Although t3 also matches send_attempt when opted_out is true, t5 also matches when ticks>=3, and t5's existence means there is an execution where it fires. Concrete reachable counterexample within the 7-event bound: (1) optout fires t1 -> opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=0; (2) tick fires t4 -> ticks=1; (3) tick fires t4 -> ticks=2; (4) tick fires t4 -> ticks=3; (5) send_attempt with opted_out=true and ticks>=3 fires t5 -> sent:=true. Resulting state opted_out=true, sent=true, ticks=3 violates opted_out implies not sent.
